title: Best National Parks in Costa Rica for Wildlife: A 2026 Guide to the country's Most Rewarding Protected Areas
excerpt: Costa Rica's national park system protects an extraordinary range of ecosystems, from cloud forest and lowland rainforest to coastal marine environments and volcanic crater landscapes. This guide covers the parks most worth your time, organized by what makes each one distinctive.
content:
Best National Parks in Costa Rica for Wildlife: A 2026 Guide to the Country's Most Rewarding Protected Areas

Costa Rica protects approximately 30% of its national territory across a network of national parks, wildlife refuges, and biological reserves. That commitment to conservation has made the country one of the most biodiverse destinations on the planet per square kilometer, and it is the reason that wildlife viewing here produces results that would be exceptional anywhere. I have visited all of the major parks multiple times over the years I have lived here, and the system continues to reward repeat visits.

Corcovado National Park — The Undisputed Crown Jewel

Every serious naturalist who visits Costa Rica eventually makes their way to Corcovado, and the experience tends to justify the effort involved in getting there. The park covers 425 square kilometers of lowland rainforest on the Osa Peninsula, and its biodiversity credentials are not marketing language — they are backed by decades of scientific documentation. The Osa Peninsula as a whole is estimated to contain 2.5% of all species on Earth in an area that represents roughly 0.001% of the planet's land surface.

Corcovado is home to all four of Costa Rica's monkey species, both two-toed and three-toed sloths, significant populations of the endangered Baird's tapir, healthy numbers of scarlet macaws, and cats — jaguars, pumas, and ocelots — that are present but rarely seen. The park's coastal area includes nesting beaches for sea turtles and rocky points where hammerhead sharks patrol the offshore waters.

Access requires either a multi-day hike from the Carate or Puerto Jiménez entrances, or a flight into the Sirena ranger station. The backcountry nature of the park means that wildlife encounters here feel genuinely wild rather than semi-managed, and this distinction matters for serious wildlife observers.

Manuel Antonio National Park — Convenience Meets Density

Manuel Antonio is the most visited park in Costa Rica and the one that most visitors encounter first. The park covers just 16 square kilometers, but its mix of beach, mangrove, and forest habitat produces wildlife density that rivals much larger parks. The endangered Central American squirrel monkey — one of the rarest primates in Central America — is reliably found here, as are white-faced capuchins, mantled howler monkeys, and both sloth species.

The value of Manuel Antonio lies in its accessibility. A two-hour drive from San José places you inside the park, and a well-planned day can combine a full trail circuit with a beach afternoon and a wildlife tally that would take weeks to match elsewhere. The trade-off is that the park's popularity means crowding during peak season, particularly on weekend mornings. Arriving at opening time and booking timed entry in advance are practical necessities.

Tortuguero National Park — Canals and Sea Turtles

Tortuguero on the Caribbean coast is one of the most distinctive park experiences in Costa Rica. Accessed by boat through a network of canals, the park's primary habitat is a mix of canal-side forest, swamp, and a beach that hosts one of the most important sea turtle nesting aggregations in the Caribbean. Green sea turtles nest here from July through October, with peak activity in August and September. Hawksbill and leatherback turtles also nest on the beach in smaller numbers.

The canal system that provides access to Tortuguero is itself the primary wildlife-viewing venue. Moving quietly by boat in the early morning produces encounters with caimans, river otters, boat-billed herons, Amazonian horned screamers, and occasionally manatees in the deeper channels. The forest along the canal edges is home to toucans, trogons, and a variety of antbirds that respond to the boat's movement.

Monteverde Cloud Forest Reserve — The Highland Forest Experience

Monteverde sits at 1,500 meters elevation in the Tilarán Mountains, where the persistent cloud immersion creates a forest environment fundamentally different from the lowland rainforests. The cloud forest's character is defined by the epiphytes — orchids, bromeliads, ferns, and a remarkable diversity of mosses — that cover every available surface from the ground up into the canopy. The forest is perpetually damp, and the quality of light filtering through the epiphyte-laden canopy produces an atmosphere unlike anything in the drier lowlands.

Monteverde's bird list exceeds 400 species, including the Golden-browed Phyllos, Three-wattled Bellbird, and a dozen hummingbird species that visit the area's feeders and gardens. The quetzal, arguably Central America's most iconic bird, is present in Monteverde during the breeding season from March through June, though seeing one requires patience and a knowledgeable guide.

Arenal Volcano National Park — Volcanic Landscape and Wildlife

Arenal Volcano National Park surrounds Costa Rica's most iconic volcanic cone and protects a mix of forest that was severely affected by the 1968 eruption and has since regenerated into dense secondary growth. The park's trails offer close views of the volcano's lower slopes and access to lava fields from the 1992 eruption that are slowly being reclaimed by vegetation.

Wildlife in the park is less dense than in Corcovado or Manuel Antonio, but the combination of the volcano as a backdrop with a secondary forest that supports capuchins, howlers, coatis, and a good variety of birds produces a distinctive experience. The park is most useful as a half-day visit combined with the Tabacón hot springs or the Venado cave experience in the surrounding area.

Cahuita and Gandoca-Manzanillo — The Caribbean Coast Parks

The two parks on the southern Caribbean coast offer contrasting experiences. Cahuita National Park is the more accessible of the two — a gently undulating trail through forest that runs to a long beach. White-faced capuchins, sloths, and toucans are regular sightings. The reef off Cahuita's beach is one of the few living coral reefs on Costa Rica's Caribbean coast and supports a variety of reef fish and marine life.

Gandoca-Manzanillo to the south is more remote and less visited. The trail is less developed, the forest more mature, and the wildlife correspondingly less habituated to human presence. For visitors who want a genuine wilderness experience on the Caribbean coast without the multi-day commitment that Corcovado requires, Gandoca-Manzanillo is a strong option.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which national park in Costa Rica is best for wildlife photography?

Corcovado for species diversity and the chance of seeing rare animals including Baird's tapir and wild cats. Manuel Antonio for the highest wildlife density per hour and the most reliable sightings of monkeys and sloths. Tortuguero for a unique canal-based wildlife experience with excellent birding. Monteverde for cloud forest specialties including quetzals during breeding season.

Do I need advance reservations for Costa Rica's national parks?

For Manuel Antonio, yes — timed entry is limited and peak-season weekends fill quickly. For Corcovado, particularly if you plan to stay at Sirena station, advance booking is essential as capacity is very limited. For Tortuguero, bookings for boat transport and accommodation in the village should be made in advance during high season. For most other parks, same-day visits are generally possible but advance planning is still advisable during December through March.

What is the best national park for beginners or families with children?

Manuel Antonio is the most accessible and family-friendly option, with well-maintained trails, safe swimming beaches, and reliable wildlife. Cahuita also works well for families, with a shorter, easier trail and the beach as a reward at the end. Both are more manageable for children than the multi-day backcountry commitment required by Corcovado.

How much does it cost to visit Costa Rica's national parks?

Park entrance fees range from approximately $10 to $15 per foreign visitor for most parks. Corcovado charges a higher fee that includes camping or accommodation at Sirena. Additional costs include guide fees where required, boat transport to Tortuguero, and any accommodation in nearby towns.

Which parks are best visited during the green season?

Most parks are actually better visited during the green season for wildlife viewing — trails are muddier but animals are more active, amphibians are breeding, and the forest is at its most lush. The exception is Tortuguero, which receives very heavy rainfall in October and November. Corcovado is best from December through April for trail conditions, though wildlife viewing is possible year-round.
